notable.notable_blurb: U.S. Forest Service forester, WWI Major in the

career.notes: WWI commission as Major. The 10th Engineers were organized to produce timber in France for the American Expeditionary Forces; his duties included purchasing standing timber for manufacture into AEF use.
Post-WWI returned to CT as consulting forester and Yale forestry school lecturer; worked with national forestry organizations and conservation groups.

military_service.notes: Forestry timber procurement for AEF in France

narrative_blocks.header: WWI Major procuring timber in France for AEF
narrative_blocks.body: In 1917 he was commissioned Major in the 10th Engineers (Forestry) — a unit organized to produce timber in France for the American Expeditionary Forces. His duties included purchasing standing timber for manufacture into AEF use; he served until 1919 then returned to Connecticut as a consulting forester and Yale forestry school lecturer.

narrative_blocks.header: Author of American Forest Regulation textbook
narrative_blocks.body: A Yale forestry lecturer and consulting forester, he wrote 'American Forest Regulation' along with other books and articles on forestry and forest regulation. His work bridged the practical forestry of the U.S. Forest Service and the academic forestry program Yale pioneered.

narrative_blocks.header: Mount Woolsey named for him after death
narrative_blocks.body: He died by self-inflicted gunshot on 10 July 1933 at fifty-three. Days later, his daughter Betty Woolsey was on a climbing party in Wyoming's Bighorn Mountains; on hearing of his death she left for New Haven, and the remaining four climbers named the unclimbed peak they then summited Mount Woolsey in his memory. The name was formally approved in 1961.

burial.plot_notes: Trail Creek Ranch, Wilson WY — memorial service held at the ranch the summer after her death

education.school_name: Educated by French governess (no formal early school)
education.notes: Family travels in California, France, Switzerland, Italy with French governess.
Vassar after family relocated to New Haven.

career.role: U.S. National Downhill Champion (Mt Rainier WA)
career.notes: Won as captain of the American women's team.

career.role: Captain, U.S. Women's Olympic Ski Team
career.notes: First Winter Olympics to feature alpine skiing. Led 6-woman U.S. team in women's combined; finished 19th of 29 with 69.24 points and was leading U.S. finisher. Later described as 'Nazi Olympics.'

career.role: U.S. National Downhill Champion (Mt Hood OR)
First editor of Ski Illustrated magazine

career.organization: Trail Creek Ranch (dude ranch + ski center)
career.notes: Bought Trail Creek Ranch 1942; opened as ski center + dude ranch 1946. Pioneered recreational alpine skiing on Teton Pass shuttling enthusiasts up + leading powder descents. Established Jackson Hole's first Nordic ski track. Base for Olympic biathlon tryouts and Junior Nordic National Championships.

cross_connections.display_label: Niece Elizabeth Woolsey La Farge bearing the same given name
Distant cousin who won the Women's Western Golf Association four years running

narrative_blocks.header: Captained U.S. women's ski team, 1936 Olympics
narrative_blocks.body: She captained the inaugural U.S. women's Olympic ski team at the 1936 Winter Olympics in Garmisch-Partenkirchen Germany — the first Games to feature alpine skiing events. She led 6 American women in the women's combined and finished 19th of 29 as leading U.S. finisher; later she described the experience as skiing in the 'Nazi Olympics.'

narrative_blocks.header: Two national downhill championships, five years apart
narrative_blocks.body: She won the US National Downhill Championship at Mount Rainier WA in 1934 and again at Mount Hood OR in 1939 — bracketing her 1936 Olympic captaincy. Between them she joined the inaugural US Women's FIS Team at Mürren Switzerland 1935, racing world championships against elite European fields.

narrative_blocks.header: Named Bighorn peaks, climbing day father died
narrative_blocks.body: In July 1933 she was climbing in Wyoming's Bighorn Mountains with William and Alan Willcox when news arrived of her father's death; she left at once for New Haven. The four remaining climbers made the first ascent of an unclimbed peak and named it Mount Woolsey in her father's memory.

narrative_blocks.header: Founded Trail Creek Ranch in Jackson Hole, 1942
narrative_blocks.body: Disillusioned with resort skiing she settled at Jackson Hole WY in 1942, buying Trail Creek Ranch at the base of Teton Pass and opening it as a dude ranch + ski center by 1946. She pioneered recreational alpine skiing on Teton Pass and established the valley's first Nordic ski track — the seed of Jackson Hole Ski Club's Nordic program.

narrative_blocks.header: Edited Ski Illustrated, wrote Off the Beaten Track
narrative_blocks.body: During NYC winters she served as the first editor of Ski Illustrated magazine 1943-1946, contributing to the sport's media growth in the U.S. Her 1984 autobiography 'Off the Beaten Track' documented her European Alps climbs, Olympic experience, and Jackson Hole ranching life.

marriages.spouse_name: Edward Hooper LaFarge (1901-1981), artist grandson of John LaFarge; RISD professor 30 yrs; m. 12 Jun 1935 Foxon East Haven CT; 7 children — Edward T, Natalie Goss, Anne Culman, Patricia Knopp, Elizabeth LaFarge, Margaret Pinsky, Catherine England

burial.plot_notes: Saint Columba Catholic Cemetery, Middletown RI — with father Bancel + mother Mabel + grandfather John

marriages.officiant: Rev. John La Farge S.J. (the bridegroom's uncle, New York City)

career.organization: Nicholas Roosevelt, U.S. Ambassador to Hungary
career.notes: Ambassador appointed by President Franklin Delano Roosevelt.
30 years as art instructor at RISD.

narrative_blocks.header: RISD art professor for thirty years
narrative_blocks.body: Grandson of the artist John La Farge and son of muralist Bancel La Farge, he graduated Choate then attended Harvard. Before teaching he served as private secretary to Nicholas Roosevelt — FDR's Ambassador to Hungary — then taught art at the Rhode Island School of Design for approximately thirty years (c.1944-1974).

narrative_blocks.header: Third generation in a New York art dynasty
narrative_blocks.body: His grandfather John La Farge pioneered American mural painting and stained glass; his father Bancel La Farge designed religious mosaics and altarpieces; he himself taught art at RISD for thirty years. The three generations spanned from the founding of the modern American mural movement at Trinity Church Boston 1878 to the post-war art school era of the 1970s.

sources.label: Smithsonian Archives of American Art — Edward H. La Farge Papers

narrative_blocks.header: Only son, present at sister's death
narrative_blocks.body: The only son of Edward Hooper La Farge and Anne Woolsey La Farge among seven children — six sisters. Resided Washington DC; present with his wife at his sister Elizabeth's January 2026 death in Quakertown PA. Carries the male La Farge line forward into the next generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Sole male heir of his La Farge line
narrative_blocks.body: As the only son among Anne Woolsey La Farge's seven children, he carries forward the male La Farge surname — descended from 19th-century American Symbolist painter John La Farge through his grandfather Bancel La Farge. Resided Washington DC at his mother's 2005 death and was present with his wife at his sister Elizabeth's January 2026 Quakertown PA death — the most recent documented Hooker descendant family event in this tree.

narrative_blocks.header: Settled near Aunt Betty's Wyoming ranch
narrative_blocks.body: She settled at Jackson WY — near her aunt Elizabeth 'Betty' Davenport Woolsey's Trail Creek Ranch at Wilson WY. The family's WY ties through the Olympic skier aunt's ranch continued through Natalie's lifetime settlement in Jackson Hole.

narrative_blocks.header: Sports Illustrated 1958 with sister Elizabeth
narrative_blocks.body: Known as 'Sita,' she was featured at age c.13 alongside her younger sister Elizabeth in a 1958 Sports Illustrated article about Aunt Betty Woolsey's Trail Creek Ranch at Wilson WY. She married Peter William Culman of Baltimore MD (predeceased her) and spoke for the family in her mother's 2005 newspaper obituary.

narrative_blocks.header: Settled in New York City
narrative_blocks.body: Resided New York City at the time of her mother's 2005 obituary. One of seven children of Edward Hooper La Farge and Anne Woolsey La Farge; great-great-granddaughter of Yale President Theodore Dwight Woolsey through her mother's Salisbury Woolsey line.

education.notes: Pennsylvania. Manager of field hockey team + competed in tennis, swimming, archery, golf. Introduced the college to skiing and started its ski club.

cross_connections.display_label: Aunt Betty Davenport Woolsey, 1936 US Olympic skier captain

narrative_blocks.header: Named for her Olympic skier aunt
narrative_blocks.body: Born March 1947 in Providence RI, she was named after her aunt Elizabeth 'Betty' Davenport Woolsey of Trail Creek Ranch in Wilson WY — captain of the 1936 US women's Olympic ski team at Garmisch and US National Champion. The name carried both the Woolsey lineage (Yale president) and the mountaineer aunt's frontier spirit.

narrative_blocks.header: Sports Illustrated 1958 at Trail Creek Ranch
narrative_blocks.body: She spent several summers at Aunt Betty's Trail Creek Ranch in Wilson WY. In 1958 at age 11, she and her sister Anne 'Sita' were featured in a Sports Illustrated article about the ranch — an early national-magazine appearance tying her to the Woolsey ski-and-mountain tradition.

narrative_blocks.header: Athlete and ski-club founder at Bucks County
narrative_blocks.body: She graduated Bucks County Community College in Pennsylvania in 1976 after attending Providence's Lincoln + St. Margaret's + St. Xavier's schools. At Bucks she managed the field hockey team and competed in tennis, swimming, archery, and golf — but her standout contribution was introducing skiing to the college and starting its ski club.

narrative_blocks.header: Tuckernuck Island summers, blackberry preserves
narrative_blocks.body: Three weeks of every summer were spent at the family's Tuckernuck Island homestead off Nantucket — swimming, exploring the island, and making blackberry and rose hip preserves. Her impeccable memory for detail and love of travel kept her connected to siblings, nieces, nephews, and cousins across two continents until her January 2026 death at 78.

sources.label: Obituary, Elizabeth Woolsey La Farge, Quakertown PA, January 2026
Obituary, Anne Salisbury Woolsey La Farge, Middletown CT, April 2005
Sports Illustrated 1958 article on Trail Creek Ranch Wilson WY

narrative_blocks.header: Settled at Orono Maine
narrative_blocks.body: Resided Orono ME at the time of her mother's 2005 obituary — Orono being the seat of the University of Maine. The family settled in academic communities across the US, reflecting the multi-generational Woolsey-La Farge intellectual tradition.

narrative_blocks.header: Only sibling to settle in Canada
narrative_blocks.body: She settled at Edmonton, Alberta, Canada — the only of Anne and Edward La Farge's seven children to leave the United States. The youngest in the 1958 Sports Illustrated photo generation, she carried the family across the northern border for the next generation of Mayflower-descended Canadians.

birth.country: Switzerland or France (per Wikipedia: 2 daughters born abroad)
